points.text: COVID-19 spreads mainly through inhaling air contaminated by droplets and aerosols from infected individuals.
Infected individuals exhale virus-containing particles when they breathe, talk, cough, sneeze, or sing.
Transmission is most likely at close range but can occur over longer distances, especially indoors.
COVID-19 can also spread when infectious particles come into contact with the eyes, nose, or mouth.
Touching surfaces contaminated with the virus and then touching the face can also lead to transmission.
People can remain contagious for up to 20 days and may spread the virus without showing symptoms.
COVID-19 spreads mainly from person to person through close contact.
The virus can spread through tiny droplets when an infected person coughs, sneezes, or talks.
Infected individuals may spread the virus even if they have no symptoms.
Droplets from an infected person's breath can land on nearby individuals.
COVID-19 can spread in areas with low airflow, increasing risk of transmission.
Risk factors include spending time in crowded spaces with poor airflow.
Close contact, such as sitting next to someone or sharing a room, increases risk.
The duration of contact with an infected person affects the likelihood of transmission.
The virus can spread from an infected person’s mouth or nose in small droplets when they cough, sneeze, speak, sing or breathe.
Particles can range in size from larger respiratory droplets to smaller aerosols, and people can be contagious without symptoms.
People may also become infected by touching their mouth, nose or eyes after touching surfaces contaminated with the virus.
